title: The ethics of inheriting a family business you don't want
summary: Explore the bind of being expected to take over a family business that doesn't fit your life.
topic: The ethics of inheriting a family business you don't want
prompt: Write an essay (350–500 words) exploring the ethics of refusing to inherit a family business. Use a concession, a cleft sentence, and a concrete international example. Avoid moralising — name the bind from both sides.
hintStarters: The bakery on a side street in Bologna…
What an inheritance asks of you is…
Granted, refusing to take over has its own cost.

hintVocab.meaningVi: đóng cửa dần / kết thúc dần
cảm thấy bị dồn vào chân tường

sampleText:
The bakery on a side street in Bologna had been in the same family since 1894. When the last baker retired in 2019, he had two grown daughters: one was a translator in Berlin, the other a paediatric nurse in Milan. Neither of them wanted to come home and learn how to fold sfogliatelle at five in the morning. The bakery closed. Photographs of it now appear in local guidebooks, captioned with a faint sadness, as if the daughters had broken something they were given to hold.

That caption deserves to be questioned. What an inheritance asks of you is not just labour but a particular shape of life — early hours, neighbourhood loyalty, an income tied to weather and tourism — and there is no moral logic that says the next generation was born to live that shape. A daughter who becomes a nurse has not stolen anything from her father. She has simply spent her one life on something else.

Granted, refusing to take over has its own cost, and pretending otherwise would be dishonest. A small business is not just an asset; it is a network of customers, suppliers, and neighbours who relied on it. When the bakery shuts, the elderly widow who came in for a single bread roll for thirty years has to walk further or eat less bread. The estrangement that follows a refusal is rarely loud — no shouting, no break — but it carries quietly through family meals for years. To opt out is also to disappoint.

What's more, the choice is almost never as clean as either side pretends. Some heirs accept the business out of love and discover, halfway through their fifties, that they are unsuited to it and resent both the work and the parent who handed it on. Others refuse, build a different life, and feel a small, unliftable guilt every time they pass a bakery that smells right. Neither outcome is unmixed. Neither is dishonourable.

Perhaps the honest position is this: a family business should be offered, not imposed, and the person offering it should design the offer in a way that the refusal is not a wound. That is hard. It may well require the parent to plan, years in advance, for a sale, a wind-down, or a non-family successor — and to grieve the family-only future before forcing it on someone who never asked for it. The daughters in Bologna did not destroy the bakery. The bakery was always going to need either willing hands or a graceful exit, and graceful exits, like inheritances, have to be designed by the generation handing them on.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to redefine the burden
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What an inheritance asks of you is not just labour but a particular shape of life' dùng cấu trúc what-cleft với 'not just X but Y' để mở rộng định nghĩa của một khái niệm.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What X asks of you is not just Y but Z' uses a what-cleft to expand a definition — moving from the obvious answer (labour) to a deeper one (shape of life).

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal hedging with 'may well require'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It may well require the parent to plan…' dùng 'may well + base verb' để đưa ra một dự đoán có giảm nhẹ — tránh nói quá tuyệt đối.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'May well + base verb' is a hedged modal that softens a prediction without weakening the argument.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tiệm bánh trên một con phố nhỏ ở Bologna đã thuộc về cùng một gia đình từ năm 1894. Khi người thợ làm bánh cuối cùng nghỉ hưu năm 2019, ông có hai con gái đã trưởng thành: một là phiên dịch ở Berlin, người kia là y tá nhi khoa ở Milan. Không ai trong số họ muốn về nhà và học cách gấp bánh sfogliatelle lúc năm giờ sáng. Tiệm bánh đóng cửa. Ảnh của nó giờ xuất hiện trong sách hướng dẫn địa phương, được chú thích với một nỗi buồn mờ nhạt, như thể những người con gái đã làm vỡ thứ mà họ được giao giữ.

Lời chú thích đó đáng được đặt câu hỏi. Điều mà một di sản đòi hỏi ở bạn không chỉ là lao động mà là một hình dạng cuộc sống cụ thể — giờ giấc dậy sớm, lòng trung thành với khu phố, một thu nhập gắn với thời tiết và du lịch — và không có logic đạo đức nào nói rằng thế hệ kế tiếp sinh ra để sống hình dạng đó. Một người con gái trở thành y tá không hề lấy cắp điều gì của cha mình. Cô chỉ đơn giản đã dành cuộc đời duy nhất của mình cho thứ khác.

Công nhận, từ chối tiếp quản cũng có cái giá của nó, và giả vờ rằng không phải vậy sẽ là không trung thực. Một doanh nghiệp nhỏ không chỉ là một tài sản; nó là một mạng lưới khách hàng, nhà cung cấp, và hàng xóm đã dựa vào nó. Khi tiệm bánh đóng, bà cụ vẫn đến mua một ổ bánh duy nhất suốt ba mươi năm phải đi xa hơn hoặc ăn ít bánh hơn. Sự xa cách theo sau một lần từ chối hiếm khi lớn tiếng — không quát tháo, không đoạn tuyệt — nhưng nó lặng lẽ trôi qua những bữa cơm gia đình trong nhiều năm. Chọn không tham gia cũng là làm thất vọng.

Hơn nữa, lựa chọn hiếm khi sạch sẽ như cả hai phía giả vờ. Một số người kế thừa nhận công việc vì tình yêu và phát hiện, đến giữa tuổi năm mươi, rằng họ không phù hợp với nó và oán cả công việc lẫn người cha đã trao nó cho mình. Người khác từ chối, xây một cuộc đời khác, và cảm thấy một nỗi tội lỗi nhỏ không thể nhấc bỏ mỗi khi đi ngang qua một tiệm bánh có mùi đúng. Không kết cục nào là thuần khiết. Không kết cục nào là đáng xấu hổ.

Có lẽ vị trí trung thực là thế này: một doanh nghiệp gia đình nên được đề nghị, không áp đặt, và người đề nghị nên thiết kế lời đề nghị sao cho việc từ chối không phải là một vết thương. Điều đó khó. Rất có thể đòi hỏi người cha mẹ phải lên kế hoạch, nhiều năm trước, cho một việc bán, một sự đóng cửa dần, hoặc một người kế nhiệm không thuộc gia đình — và đau buồn cho cái tương lai chỉ thuộc về gia đình trước khi ép nó lên một người chưa từng xin nó. Hai cô con gái ở Bologna không phá hủy tiệm bánh. Tiệm bánh dù sao cũng cần hoặc bàn tay sẵn lòng hoặc một lối thoát duyên dáng, và những lối thoát duyên dáng, cũng như di sản, phải được thiết kế bởi thế hệ trao chúng lại.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which what-cleft expands a definition by moving from a simple answer to a deeper one?
options: What an inheritance asks of you is not just labour but a particular shape of life.
Inheritance what asks of you the labour life shape just particular.
It is what an inheritance that the labour shape life particular.
Asks of you what an inheritance the labour just particular life shape.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X asks of you is not just Y but Z' uses the cleft to broaden the meaning.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is not just … but …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which concession opens with the cleanest B2 marker?
options: Granted, refusing to take over has its own cost, and pretending otherwise would be dishonest.
Of course granted refusing take over cost own dishonest pretending.
Conceding granted otherwise refusing pretending dishonest cost.
Even granted dishonest otherwise refuse take over its cost.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'Granted,' followed by a fluent clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'rarely' with inversion correctly?
options: The estrangement that follows a refusal is rarely loud — no shouting, no break.
Rarely it the estrangement is loud — no shouting break.
Loud rarely the estrangement follows refusal no shouting break.
Estrangement rarely no shouting break it loud follows refusal.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option A places 'rarely' in normal position; the others scramble word order.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Rarely' between auxiliary/copula and the rest of the predicate.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure correctly?
options: Some heirs accept the business out of love and discover, halfway through their fifties, that they are unsuited to it.
Some heirs the business out of love accepting discover halfway fifties unsuited it.
Accepting some heirs business love halfway discover their fifties unsuited.
Out of love heirs some accepting halfway discover fifties unsuited their.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option A keeps two parallel finite verbs ('accept … discover …') with parenthetical detail.
mcQuestions.hint: Two coordinated verbs around the same subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'It may well require' correctly to hedge a prediction?
options: It may well require the parent to plan, years in advance, for a sale, a wind-down, or a non-family successor.
Well it may require the parent plan years advance sale wind-down.
Require it well may the parent advance plan wind-down years sale.
Plan the parent may well it require sale advance years wind-down.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It may well require + object + to-infinitive' is the natural hedged form.
mcQuestions.hint: It + may well + require + object + to-infinitive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option adds a related point cohesively?
options: Anyway moreover, the choice is almost never as clean as either side pretends.
What's more, the choice is almost never as clean as either side pretends.
In conclusion the choice is never clean side.
Throughout history all choices have not been clean either side.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What's more, …' is the cleanest cohesive marker for adding a related point.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a relative clause correctly?
options: The elderly widow who came in for a single bread roll for thirty years has to walk further.
The elderly widow which a single bread roll for thirty years coming in walk further.
Widow elderly that for a single bread roll thirty years has walk further coming.
Walking further the elderly widow who coming in bread roll thirty years a single.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Who came in for X…' attaches a defining clause cleanly to 'widow'.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clause with 'who' for a person.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option keeps a B2 register and avoids cliché?
options: In today's modern society, inheriting is hard.
Throughout history, families have argued.
The estrangement that follows a refusal is rarely loud — no shouting, no break — but it carries quietly through family meals for years.
It is important to note that refusing inheritance is bad.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C is specific, considered, and avoids cliché.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the option with concrete texture.

options: to wind down a business
to wind down a sandwich
to wind down a kerb
to wind down a footfall
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Wind down a business' is the natural phrasal-verb-plus-object collocation.
mcQuestions.hint: Which one fits a business context?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'neither' twice correctly to balance two judgements?
options: Neither outcome is unmixed. Neither is dishonourable.
Either outcome neither unmixed. Neither either dishonourable.
Neither it is unmixed outcome. Dishonourable neither it.
It neither outcome unmixed is. Dishonourable it neither.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Neither outcome is X. Neither is Y.' uses parallel sentence-initial negation cleanly.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for two short sentences each starting 'Neither …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option introduces the writer's tentative position cleanly?
options: Perhaps the honest position is this: a family business should be offered, not imposed.
Honest perhaps the position business family offered imposed.
Position the honest perhaps offered family imposed business.
It perhaps honest the position business family offered imposed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Perhaps the honest position is this: + clause' uses the colon to introduce the formulation.

options: Feel a small, unliftable guilt every time they pass a bakery that smells right.
They feel a small, unliftable guilt every time they pass a bakery that smells right.
Feeling small unliftable guilt they pass a bakery smell right.
Pass a bakery that smell right they feel small unliftable guilt.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is the full grammatical sentence; the others omit the subject or scramble word order.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which ending avoids moralising while still landing the argument?
options: We must all be grateful for what our parents leave us, no matter what.
In conclusion, inheritance is a difficult problem.
The bakery was always going to need either willing hands or a graceful exit, and graceful exits, like inheritances, have to be designed by the generation handing them on.
Throughout history, all family businesses have ended this way.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C ends inside the argument with a concrete formulation rather than a sermon.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the ending that finishes the argument, not preaches it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a clean 'should be + past participle' passive?
options: A family business should be offered, not imposed.
A family business offered should be, not imposed.
Should a family business be offered, imposed not it.
Family business it should offer, not impose be.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Should be + past participle' is the standard modal passive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence avoids fake balance and admits real cost on both sides?
options: Both sides are equally right, equally wrong, and equally tired.

Some heirs accept the business out of love and discover, halfway through their fifties, that they are unsuited to it and resent both the work and the parent who handed it on. Others refuse, build a different life, and feel a small, unliftable guilt every time they pass a bakery that smells right.
Refusing inheritance is fine; accepting it is fine; everything is fine.
In conclusion, both sides have their points.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B names the specific cost of each path without pretending they are symmetric platitudes.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the option that names both costs concretely.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If you were offered a family business tomorrow that didn't fit your life, what conditions would let you accept it without resentment — and would the person offering it accept those conditions?
